description: Early church order document covering baptism, fasting, Eucharist, itinerant prophets, and the Lord's Prayer.
significance: Earliest description of Eucharistic prayer and Sunday assembly practice; key source for early liturgy and church order.

description: Letter from the church of Rome to the church at Corinth addressing leadership disputes and urging order.
significance: Earliest extant letter from a Roman bishop asserting authority over another church; establishes liturgical order and apostolic succession themes.

description: Written en route to martyrdom in Rome; addresses docetism directly and uses the term "Catholic Church" for the first time.
significance: First extant use of "Catholic Church"; clearest early statement of the real bodily resurrection of Christ; attestation of Eucharist as "flesh of Christ."

description: Written en route to martyrdom; Ignatius begs the Roman church not to prevent his martyrdom.
significance: Attestation of Roman church's prestige and authority; earliest expression of martyrdom theology as union with Christ.

description: Roman governor Pliny asks Emperor Trajan how to handle Christians in Bithynia; describes early Christian practice and the emperor's response.
significance: The earliest non-Christian eyewitness account of Christian worship; attests Sunday hymn-singing, communal meal, and oath-taking.

description: Addressed to Emperor Antoninus Pius defending Christianity as philosophically superior to paganism; includes detailed description of Sunday Eucharist and baptism.
significance: First systematic description of Sunday Eucharist; earliest description of baptism rite; defense of Christian ethics.

description: Five-book refutation of Gnostic systems, particularly Valentinianism; establishes the rule of faith and apostolic succession.
significance: Most complete early refutation of Gnosticism; establishes apostolic succession doctrine; preserves Gnostic texts now lost.

description: Legal-argument approach to refuting heresy; heretics have no right to appeal to Scripture since the churches hold apostolic tradition.
significance: Foundational text for the argument from apostolic tradition against private interpretation; coined key Latin theological terms.

description: First systematic Christian theology covering God, Christ, the Holy Spirit, creation, free will, and Scripture interpretation.
significance: First attempt at a comprehensive dogmatic theology; enormously influential despite later condemnation of some positions.

description: Ten-book chronicle of Christianity from the apostles through Constantine; preserves numerous otherwise lost documents and letters.
significance: The primary surviving narrative history of the first three centuries; indispensable source for all early church historians.

description: The dogmatic statement produced by the First Council of Nicaea defining Christ as homoousios (consubstantial) with the Father.
significance: Foundational creedal statement of Trinitarian orthodoxy; still used across Catholic, Orthodox, and most Protestant traditions.

description: Theological defense of the Incarnation explaining why God became man; written before Nicaea while Athanasius was a deacon.
significance: Classic text on the Incarnation that became the theological backbone of Nicene Christology; widely read across traditions.

description: Autobiographical spiritual journey from Manichaeism through skepticism to Christian conversion; foundational text in Western self-examination.
significance: First Western autobiography; shaped Western theology of grace, conversion, and interiority; widely read across all Christian traditions.

description: Twenty-two book defense of Christianity written after the sack of Rome (410); develops the "two cities" theology of history.
significance: Foundational for Western political theology; shaped medieval understanding of church-state relations; hugely influential.

description: Church order document describing Roman liturgical practice including the earliest complete Eucharistic prayer, ordination rites, and baptismal liturgy.
significance: Most important source for early 3rd-century Roman liturgy; the Eucharistic prayer influenced all later anaphoras.

description: Treatise on church unity arguing that the episcopate is one, held by each bishop for the whole; written during the Novatian schism.
significance: Foundational text for Catholic ecclesiology; established the principle that there is no salvation outside the church.

description: Biography of Anthony the Great that popularized the monastic ideal throughout the Roman world; influenced Augustine's conversion.
significance: The most influential hagiography in Christian history; directly inspired Western monasticism and Augustine.

description: Defense of the full divinity of the Holy Spirit against the Pneumatomachi; key text leading to Constantinople (381).
significance: Definitive patristic treatise on the Holy Spirit's divinity; directly influenced the Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed.

description: Five orations delivered in Constantinople defending Trinitarian orthodoxy; earned Gregory the title "The Theologian."
significance: The most celebrated patristic treatment of the Trinity; directly shaped the theology of Constantinople (381).

description: Latin translation of the Bible from Hebrew and Greek originals; commissioned by Pope Damasus I.
significance: Standard Bible of Western Christianity for over 1000 years; shaped Latin theological vocabulary; declared authoritative at Trent.

title_display: Divine Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom
description: The primary Eucharistic liturgy of Eastern Orthodoxy, attributed to Chrysostom's revision of the Antiochene rite.
significance: The most widely celebrated liturgy in Eastern Christianity; used on most Sundays and feast days.

description: Collection of 87 hymns defending Nicene orthodoxy against Arianism through poetic theology rather than philosophical argument.
significance: Most important Syriac theological poetry; demonstrates a non-Greek approach to Trinitarian theology.

description: Monastic rule establishing the framework for communal religious life: prayer, work, study, and obedience under an abbot.
significance: The foundational document of Western monasticism; "ora et labora" principle shaped European civilization.

description: Systematic theology summarizing Greek patristic thought in three parts: philosophy, theology, and Christology.
significance: Last great systematic theology of the Eastern Fathers; became the standard Orthodox dogmatic reference.

description: Apocalyptic text in three parts (Visions, Mandates, Similitudes) addressing repentance, morality, and church order in Rome.
significance: Widely read as near-canonical in the 2nd-3rd centuries; key source for understanding Roman Christian piety and penitential practice.

description: Circular letter from the church of Smyrna describing Polycarp's arrest, trial, and execution; the earliest dated martyrdom account.
significance: Established the literary genre of the martyrdom account; first attestation of a martyr's feast day.

description: Prison diary of Perpetua combined with eyewitness narrative of the martyrdom; one of the earliest texts by a Christian woman.
significance: Unique first-person female voice from the early church; key source for catechumenate, visions, and North African piety.

description: Manual for bishops on the responsibilities and temptations of pastoral leadership; widely circulated in the medieval West.
significance: The most influential guide to pastoral care until the Reformation; required reading for medieval bishops.
